battle-koan-1281.gloss: A huge Yuan invasion force failed to break Japanese defenses and was wrecked by storms. The battle became central to the kamikaze tradition in Japanese memory.

battle-falkirk-1298.gloss: Edward I defeated William Wallace's army at Falkirk. English longbowmen and cavalry broke the Scottish schiltrons and ended Wallace's command.

battle-morgarten-1315.gloss: Swiss forces ambushed a Habsburg army at Morgarten. The battle strengthened the early Swiss Confederation's military reputation.

series.label: Ottoman expansion in Europe and the Mediterranean

battle-maritsa-1371.gloss: Ottoman forces surprised and defeated Serbian armies near the Maritsa River. The victory accelerated Ottoman expansion into the Balkans.

battle-blue-waters-1362.gloss: Lithuanian forces defeated Golden Horde armies at Blue Waters. The battle expanded Lithuanian influence across parts of Ukraine.

battle-cerignola-1503.gloss: Spanish forces defeated a French army at Cerignola using field defenses and firearms. The battle is often cited as an early sign of gunpowder infantry's importance.

battle-ravenna-1512.gloss: French and Ferrarese forces defeated the Holy League near Ravenna but suffered heavy losses. The battle showed the destructive power of artillery and pike warfare.

battle-otumba-1520.war: Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire

series.label: Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire

battle-otumba-1520.gloss: Cortes' battered army defeated Aztec forces at Otumba after fleeing Tenochtitlan. The victory allowed the Spaniards and their allies to recover and continue the conquest.

combatants: Grand Duchy of Lithuania and Poland
battle-orsha-1514.gloss: Lithuanian and Polish forces defeated a Muscovite army near Orsha. The battle checked Muscovy's advance after the loss of Smolensk.

series.label: Portuguese expansion in the Indian Ocean

siege-diu-1538.gloss: Portuguese defenders held Diu against a major Ottoman-Gujarati siege. The defense preserved Portuguese naval positions in the western Indian Ocean.

battle-gravelines-1588.gloss: English ships attacked the Spanish Armada near Gravelines and disrupted its formation. The battle helped prevent Spain's planned invasion of England.

battle-nieuwpoort-1600.gloss: Maurice of Nassau defeated Spanish forces near Nieuwpoort. The battle demonstrated Dutch tactical reforms even though the campaign gained little strategic ground.
battle-nieuwpoort-1600.casualties: Dutch Republic and England: about 1,700-2,700 dead or wounded; Spain: about 4,000 killed, wounded, or captured

battle-jankau-1645.gloss: Swedish forces defeated an imperial army at Jankau and opened the road toward Vienna. The battle intensified pressure for a negotiated end to the Thirty Years War.

series.label: Ottoman expansion in Europe and the Mediterranean

battle-saint-gotthard-1664.gloss: A Habsburg-led army defeated Ottoman forces near Saint Gotthard. The victory checked Ottoman pressure in western Hungary.

battle-la-hogue-1692.gloss: Anglo-Dutch forces defeated the French fleet around Barfleur and La Hogue. The battle ended immediate French hopes of invading England for James II.

battle-cartagena-indias-1741.gloss: Spanish defenders repelled a large British expedition against Cartagena de Indias. The failed assault preserved Spanish control of a key Caribbean port.

battle-dettingen-1743.gloss: George II was present when the Pragmatic Army defeated French forces at Dettingen. It was the last battle in which a British monarch personally led troops.

battle-hohenfriedberg-1745.gloss: Frederick the Great defeated an Austro-Saxon army at Hohenfriedberg. The victory confirmed Prussia's battlefield strength in the Silesian wars.

battle-prague-1757.gloss: Frederick the Great defeated Austrian forces outside Prague but suffered heavy losses. The victory led to a siege that was soon undone by defeat at Kolin.

battle-kolin-1757.gloss: Austrian forces defeated Frederick the Great at Kolin. The battle forced Prussia to abandon the siege of Prague and widened the Seven Years War.

battle-karnal-1739.gloss: Nader Shah defeated the Mughal army at Karnal with a smaller force. The victory led to the sack of Delhi and exposed Mughal military decline.

combatants: British East India Company and allies
siege-seringapatam-1799.gloss: British-led forces stormed Seringapatam and killed Tipu Sultan. The siege ended Mysore's strongest resistance to British expansion in southern India.

battle-long-island-1776.gloss: British forces outflanked Washington's army on Long Island and drove it back to Brooklyn Heights. The American escape preserved the Continental Army.
battle-long-island-1776.casualties: Great Britain: 388 casualties; United States: about 2,000-2,200 casualties

battle-princeton-1777.gloss: Washington defeated British troops at Princeton after the surprise at Trenton. The victory revived American morale during a dangerous winter.
battle-princeton-1777.casualties: United States: about 40 casualties; Great Britain: about 275 casualties

battle-monmouth-1778.gloss: The Continental Army fought the British to a tactical draw at Monmouth. The battle showed the improved discipline of Washington's army after Valley Forge.

battle-camden-1780.gloss: British forces under Cornwallis routed an American army at Camden. The defeat deepened the crisis for Patriot forces in the South.
battle-camden-1780.casualties: Great Britain: 324 casualties; United States: about 1,900 casualties

battle-kings-mountain-1780.gloss: Patriot militia defeated Loyalists at Kings Mountain during the southern campaign. The victory disrupted British plans to mobilize Loyalist support.
battle-kings-mountain-1780.casualties: Patriot militia: 88 casualties; Loyalist militia: about 1,100 killed, wounded, or captured

battle-eutaw-springs-1781.outcome: British tactical victory; American strategic gain
battle-eutaw-springs-1781.gloss: Greene's army fought British forces at Eutaw Springs in one of the last major southern battles. The battle further weakened British control inland.

battle-rivoli-1797.gloss: Napoleon defeated an Austrian relief attempt at Rivoli. The victory secured French control in northern Italy and confirmed his operational brilliance.

battle-nile-1798.war: French campaign in Egypt and Syria
battle-nile-1798.gloss: Nelson destroyed the French fleet at Aboukir Bay. The victory trapped Napoleon's army in Egypt and strengthened British naval dominance.

battle-hohenlinden-1800.gloss: Moreau's French army defeated Austrian and Bavarian forces at Hohenlinden. The victory helped force Austria toward peace.

battle-ulm-1805.gloss: Napoleon encircled Mack's Austrian army around Ulm and forced its surrender. The operation showed the strategic power of rapid corps maneuver.

battle-beresina-1812.gloss: Napoleon's retreating army forced a crossing of the Berezina under pressure from Russian forces. The battle saved part of the army while underscoring the catastrophe of the Russian campaign.

battle-bautzen-1813.gloss: Napoleon defeated Russo-Prussian forces at Bautzen but failed to destroy them. The incomplete victory contributed to an armistice that let the coalition recover.

battle-dresden-1813.gloss: Napoleon defeated a large coalition army at Dresden. It was one of his last major victories before the strategic disaster at Leipzig.

battle-magenta-1859.gloss: French and Sardinian forces defeated Austria at Magenta. The victory helped open Lombardy during the campaign for Italian unification.

second-bull-run-1862.gloss: Robert E. Lee and Stonewall Jackson defeated John Pope's Union army at Second Bull Run. The victory opened the way for Lee's first invasion of the North.
second-bull-run-1862.casualties: Confederate States: 7,298 casualties; United States: 14,462 casualties

battle-chattanooga-1863.gloss: Union forces broke Confederate positions around Chattanooga after a series of attacks including Missionary Ridge. The victory opened the route into Georgia.
battle-chattanooga-1863.casualties: United States: 5,824 casualties; Confederate States: 6,667 casualties

battle-mobile-bay-1864.gloss: Farragut's fleet forced its way into Mobile Bay and defeated Confederate naval defenses. The victory closed one of the Confederacy's last major Gulf ports.
battle-mobile-bay-1864.casualties: United States Navy: about 320 casualties; Confederate States: about 1,500 killed, wounded, or captured

battle-mars-la-tour-1870.gloss: Prussian forces blocked the French Army of the Rhine from retreating west at Mars-la-Tour. The battle helped trap Bazaine's army around Metz.
battle-mars-la-tour-1870.casualties: Prussia: about 16,000 casualties; France: about 17,000 casualties

battle-gravelotte-1870.gloss: German armies fought the French at Gravelotte in the largest battle of the Franco-Prussian War. The result pinned the French army inside Metz.
battle-gravelotte-1870.casualties: Prussia and German allies: about 20,000 casualties; France: about 12,000 casualties

battle-worth-1870.gloss: German forces defeated MacMahon's army at Worth early in the Franco-Prussian War. The victory opened Alsace and helped set up the campaign toward Sedan.
battle-worth-1870.casualties: Prussia and German allies: about 10,600 casualties; France: about 19,000 casualties

battle-paardeberg-1900.gloss: British forces surrounded and forced the surrender of Piet Cronje's Boer army at Paardeberg. The victory shifted momentum in the Second Boer War.
battle-paardeberg-1900.casualties: British Empire: about 1,270 casualties; Boer republics: about 4,100 killed, wounded, or captured

battle-colenso-1899.gloss: Boer defenders defeated a British attempt to relieve Ladysmith at Colenso. The battle was part of Britain's humiliating Black Week.

battle-magersfontein-1899.gloss: Boer forces entrenched at the foot of hills surprised and defeated a British attack at Magersfontein. The battle deepened the crisis of Black Week.

battle-tel-el-kebir-1882.gloss: British forces launched a dawn assault on Egyptian entrenchments at Tel el-Kebir. The victory secured British control over Egypt.

battle-maiwand-1880.gloss: Ayub Khan's Afghan army defeated a British-Indian brigade near Maiwand. The defeat became one of the most famous British setbacks in Afghanistan.

siege-delhi-1857.gloss: Company forces besieged and recaptured Delhi from rebel troops during the Indian Rebellion of 1857. The fall of the city was a central turning point in the uprising.

battle-coronel-1914.gloss: German cruisers under Spee destroyed a British squadron off Chile. Coronel was Britain's first major naval defeat in more than a century.
battle-coronel-1914.casualties: German East Asia Squadron: 3 wounded; Royal Navy: 1,654 killed

battle-falkland-islands-1914.gloss: British battlecruisers destroyed Spee's squadron near the Falklands. The battle avenged Coronel and removed a major German raiding threat.

battle-dogger-bank-1915.gloss: British battlecruisers intercepted German ships at Dogger Bank. The action exposed command and signaling problems on both sides before Jutland.
battle-dogger-bank-1915.casualties: Royal Navy: 95 casualties; Imperial German Navy: about 950 casualties

battle-masurian-lakes-1914.gloss: German forces drove the Russian First Army out of East Prussia after Tannenberg. The battle stabilized Germany's northeastern frontier in 1914.

battle-st-quentin-canal-1918.gloss: Allied troops broke through the Hindenburg Line at the St Quentin Canal. The victory accelerated Germany's collapse on the Western Front.

battle-dobro-pole-1918.gloss: Allied forces broke through Bulgarian positions at Dobro Pole. The defeat pushed Bulgaria toward armistice and opened the Central Powers' Balkan flank.

battle-cer-1914.gloss: Serbian forces defeated Austria-Hungary at Cer in the first months of World War I. It was one of the first Allied victories of the war.

battle-kolubara-1914.gloss: Serbia counterattacked and drove Austro-Hungarian forces out after the Battle of Kolubara. The victory delayed Central Powers plans in the Balkans.

siege-przemysl-1914.gloss: Russian forces captured the Austro-Hungarian fortress of Przemysl after a long siege. It was one of the largest fortress surrenders of World War I.

battle-sidi-barrani-1940.gloss: British Commonwealth forces attacked Italian camps around Sidi Barrani and began Operation Compass. The victory shattered Italy's forward position in Egypt.
battle-sidi-barrani-1940.casualties: British Commonwealth forces: about 624 casualties; Italy: about 38,000 captured plus killed and wounded

battle-beda-fomm-1941.gloss: British forces cut off and captured much of Italy's retreating Tenth Army at Beda Fomm. The victory capped the stunning early success of Operation Compass.
battle-beda-fomm-1941.casualties: British Commonwealth forces: about 500 casualties; Italy: about 25,000 captured

siege-odessa-1941.gloss: Romanian and German forces besieged Soviet-held Odessa for more than two months. The defense tied down Axis forces before the city was evacuated.

siege-sevastopol-1941.gloss: German and Romanian forces captured Sevastopol after a long siege and massive artillery bombardment. The battle secured Crimea for the Axis for a time.

battle-kharkov-1942.gloss: A Soviet offensive near Kharkov was encircled and destroyed by German forces. The defeat weakened Soviet armies before Germany's 1942 summer offensive.

battle-korsun-1944.gloss: Soviet forces encircled German troops in the Korsun-Cherkassy pocket. Many Germans broke out, but the battle continued the Wehrmacht's retreat in Ukraine.

series.label: World War II in Asia and the Pacific

battle-tinian-1944.gloss: US forces captured Tinian after an efficient amphibious landing. The island later became a major B-29 base for attacks on Japan.
battle-tinian-1944.casualties: United States: 328 killed and 1,571 wounded; Empire of Japan: about 8,000 killed and 300 captured

series.label: World War II in Asia and the Pacific

battle-makin-1943.gloss: US forces captured Makin during the Gilbert Islands campaign. The battle accompanied the more costly landing at Tarawa.

series.label: World War II in Asia and the Pacific

battle-eastern-solomons-1942.gloss: American and Japanese carrier forces clashed during the Guadalcanal campaign. The battle helped prevent Japanese reinforcement of the island.
battle-eastern-solomons-1942.casualties: Allied naval forces: about 90 killed; Imperial Japanese Navy: about 290 killed

series.label: World War II in Asia and the Pacific

battle-santa-cruz-islands-1942.gloss: Japanese carriers inflicted heavy losses on the US Navy near the Santa Cruz Islands. The battle cost Japan experienced aircrews it could not easily replace.
battle-santa-cruz-islands-1942.casualties: Imperial Japanese Navy: about 400-500 killed; United States Navy: 266 killed

series.label: World War II in Asia and the Pacific

naval-guadalcanal-1942.gloss: US and Japanese warships fought a brutal series of night actions off Guadalcanal. The American victory helped end Japan's effort to retake Henderson Field.
naval-guadalcanal-1942.casualties: United States Navy: about 1,700 killed; Imperial Japanese Navy: about 1,900 killed

series.label: World War II in Asia and the Pacific

battle-cape-esperance-1942.gloss: American cruisers and destroyers intercepted Japanese ships near Cape Esperance. The action was an early US surface victory in the Guadalcanal campaign.

series.label: World War II in Asia and the Pacific

battle-luzon-1945.gloss: Allied forces landed on Luzon and fought to liberate the Philippines' main island. The campaign included Manila and prolonged fighting in the mountains.

battle-rimini-1944.gloss: Allied forces broke through toward Rimini during attacks on the Gothic Line. The battle was one of the heaviest fought in Italy in 1944.

battle-pusan-perimeter-1950.gloss: UN and South Korean forces held a shrinking perimeter around Pusan against North Korean attacks. The defense preserved a foothold for the Inchon counteroffensive.
battle-pusan-perimeter-1950.casualties: United Nations Command: about 60,000 casualties; North Korea: about 58,000 casualties

battle-kapyong-1951.gloss: Australian, Canadian, New Zealand, and other UN forces stopped Chinese attacks near Kapyong. The defense helped protect Seoul during the spring offensive.
battle-kapyong-1951.casualties: United Nations Command: about 100 casualties; China: about 1,000 casualties

battle-bloody-ridge-1951.gloss: UN forces captured a heavily contested ridge after costly fighting with North Korean troops. The battle reflected the Korean War's shift toward attritional hill combat.
battle-bloody-ridge-1951.casualties: United Nations Command: about 2,700 casualties; North Korea: about 15,000 casualties

battle-heartbreak-ridge-1951.gloss: UN forces fought for weeks to capture Heartbreak Ridge. The battle became a symbol of the costly stalemate phase of the Korean War.
battle-heartbreak-ridge-1951.casualties: United Nations Command: about 3,700 casualties; North Korea and China: about 25,000 casualties

combatants: South Vietnam and United States advisers
battle-ap-bac-1963.gloss: Viet Cong forces resisted a South Vietnamese operation supported by American advisers and helicopters. Ap Bac revealed serious weaknesses in South Vietnam's army.
battle-ap-bac-1963.casualties: Viet Cong: about 57 casualties; South Vietnam and United States advisers: about 220 casualties

battle-hamburger-hill-1969.outcome: American and South Vietnamese tactical victory
battle-hamburger-hill-1969.gloss: US and South Vietnamese troops captured Dong Ap Bia after repeated assaults. The battle became controversial because the hill was abandoned soon afterward.

battle-valley-tears-1973.gloss: Israeli armored units held against Syrian attacks in the Valley of Tears. The battle helped stop Syria's breakthrough on the Golan Heights.

battle-ammunition-hill-1967.gloss: Israeli paratroopers captured Ammunition Hill from Jordanian defenders during the fight for Jerusalem. The battle became one of Israel's best-known actions of the Six-Day War.

battle-mount-gaurus-343bc.gloss: Livy presents Mount Gaurus as the first Roman battle against the Samnites. The traditional Roman victory opened the First Samnite War in Campania.

battle-caudine-forks-321bc.gloss: Samnite forces trapped a Roman army at the Caudine Forks and forced its surrender. The humiliation became a defining Roman memory of the Second Samnite War.

battle-aqaba-1917.gloss: Arab Revolt forces captured Aqaba from the landward side with T. E. Lawrence advising the attack. The victory gave the revolt a Red Sea port and threatened Ottoman communications.

capture-damascus-1918.gloss: British, Indian, Australian, and Arab forces entered Damascus after the victory at Megiddo. The capture marked the collapse of Ottoman control in Syria.

sack-constantinople-1204.gloss: Fourth Crusade forces captured and sacked Constantinople, creating the Latin Empire. The event fractured Byzantine power and reshaped the eastern Mediterranean.

siege-masada-73.gloss: Roman forces under Lucius Flavius Silva besieged and captured the desert fortress of Masada, the last major rebel holdout of the First Jewish-Roman War.
siege-masada-73.casualties: Sicarii and inhabitants: about 960 dead; Roman Empire: not recorded

siege-busanjin-1592.gloss: Japanese forces captured Busanjin in the opening battle of the invasions of Korea. The fall of Busan opened the route for Japan's rapid advance into Joseon.
siege-busanjin-1592.casualties: Japan: not recorded; Joseon Korea: about 1,200-8,500 killed and 200 captured

battle-okpo-1592.gloss: Yi Sun-sin and Won Kyun destroyed Japanese transports at Okpo. It was Yi's first naval victory and began the pressure on Japanese sea logistics.
battle-okpo-1592.casualties: Toyotomi Japan: 26 transports lost; Joseon Korea: 1 wounded

battle-hansan-island-1592.gloss: Yi Sun-sin's fleet defeated Japanese naval forces near Hansan Island and Angolpo. The victory sharply limited Japanese operations along Korea's southern coast.
battle-hansan-island-1592.casualties: Toyotomi Japan: about 100 ships lost; Joseon Korea: 19 dead and 114 wounded

battle-haengju-1593.gloss: Gwon Yul's smaller Joseon force held Haengjusanseong against repeated Japanese attacks. The victory threatened Japanese positions around Hanseong.
battle-haengju-1593.casualties: Joseon Korea: not recorded; Toyotomi Japan: at least 110 casualties, possibly much higher

battle-bibracte-58bc.gloss: Caesar defeated the Helvetii-led migration near Bibracte, halting their movement through Gaul. It was the first major battle of the Gallic Wars.
battle-bibracte-58bc.casualties: Roman Republic: unknown; Helvetii-led coalition: 238,000 killed or captured according to Caesar

battle-vosges-58bc.gloss: Caesar defeated Ariovistus and his Germanic coalition near the Vosges. The victory pushed Germanic forces back toward the Rhine and deepened Roman intervention in Gaul.
battle-vosges-58bc.casualties: Roman Republic: unknown; Suebi and allies: 80,000 killed according to Caesar

battle-sabis-57bc.location: Near the Sabis River, northern Gaul
battle-sabis-57bc.gloss: The Nervii and allied Belgic tribes ambushed Caesar's army near the Sabis. Roman discipline and reinforcements turned a near disaster into a victory.
battle-sabis-57bc.casualties: Roman Republic: unknown; Nervii-led Belgae: about 59,500 casualties

battle-morbihan-56bc.gloss: Decimus Brutus defeated the Veneti fleet off Brittany after Roman crews disabled their rigging. The victory broke Venetic naval power and secured Caesar's coastal campaign.
battle-morbihan-56bc.casualties: Roman Republic: light losses; Veneti: almost all ships destroyed or captured

battle-gergovia-52bc.gloss: Caesar's attempt to storm Vercingetorix's stronghold at Gergovia collapsed after confusion near the walls. The defeat forced Caesar to retreat before the later campaign at Alesia.
battle-gergovia-52bc.casualties: Roman Republic and allies: at least 46 centurions and 700 legionaries killed; modern estimates several thousand killed; Gallic tribes: unknown

fall-granada-1492.title: Fall of Granada
combatants: Catholic Monarchs of Castile and Aragon
fall-granada-1492.gloss: Granada surrendered to Ferdinand and Isabella after the final campaigns against the Nasrid emirate. The capitulation ended Muslim rule in Iberia and closed the Reconquista.

siege-alcazar-1936.title: Siege of the Alcazar
siege-alcazar-1936.gloss: Nationalist cadets, Civil Guards, and allies held the Alcazar of Toledo against Republican militia until Franco's Army of Africa relieved it. The siege became an early propaganda victory for the Nationalists.

siege-madrid-1936.title: Siege of Madrid
siege-madrid-1936.gloss: Nationalist forces tried to seize Madrid in late 1936, but Republican defenders and International Brigades held the capital. The failed assault turned the city into a long siege until March 1939.

battle-jarama-1937.title: Battle of Jarama
battle-jarama-1937.gloss: Nationalist forces attacked east of Madrid to cut the road to Valencia. Fierce fighting, including heavy International Brigade losses, ended without the breakthrough Franco sought.

battle-guadalajara-1937.title: Battle of Guadalajara
combatants: Nationalist Spain and Italian Corpo Truppe Volontarie
battle-guadalajara-1937.gloss: Italian CTV and Nationalist troops advanced northeast of Madrid but were checked by Republican counterattacks. The Republican victory dented Italian prestige and kept pressure off the capital.

battle-brunete-1937.title: Battle of Brunete
battle-brunete-1937.gloss: The Republicans attacked west of Madrid to relieve pressure on the north. Initial gains collapsed under Nationalist counterattacks, costing both sides heavily.

battle-teruel-1937.title: Battle of Teruel
battle-teruel-1937.gloss: Republican forces captured Teruel during a brutal winter offensive, only for Nationalists to retake the city. The battle bled both armies and exposed Republican limits.

battle-ebro-1938.title: Battle of the Ebro
combatants: Nationalist Spain and Axis air forces
battle-ebro-1938.gloss: Republican armies crossed the Ebro in their last major offensive, delaying but not reversing Nationalist momentum. Their defeat opened the road to Catalonia.

siege-detroit-1812.title: Siege of Detroit
siege-detroit-1812.gloss: British and Indigenous forces under Isaac Brock and Tecumseh compelled William Hull to surrender Detroit without a major assault. The victory secured Upper Canada early in the War of 1812.

burning-washington-1814.title: Burning of Washington
burning-washington-1814.gloss: British troops entered Washington, D.C., after the American defeat at Bladensburg and burned major public buildings. The raid shocked the United States but did not end the war.

battle-baltimore-1814.title: Battle of Baltimore
battle-baltimore-1814.gloss: American defenders repelled the British attack on Baltimore and withstood the bombardment of Fort McHenry. Francis Scott Key's view of the flag inspired the poem that became the national anthem, "The Star-Spangled Banner."

battle-heiligerlee-1568.gloss: Louis of Nassau defeated a Spanish force at Heiligerlee, the first Dutch rebel battlefield victory of the Eighty Years War. Adolf of Nassau was killed in the action.
battle-heiligerlee-1568.casualties: Dutch rebels: 50 dead or wounded; Spanish Empire: 460 dead

battle-jemmingen-1568.gloss: The Duke of Alba crushed Louis of Nassau's rebel army at Jemmingen after the failed attempt to take Groningen. The defeat erased the momentum from Heiligerlee.
battle-jemmingen-1568.casualties: Dutch rebels: 7,000 dead or wounded; Spanish Empire: 8 dead and 220 wounded

siege-haarlem-1572.gloss: Haarlem surrendered after a bloody Spanish siege, and its garrison was massacred. The defense became a model of resistance for later Dutch rebel strongholds.
siege-haarlem-1572.casualties: Dutch rebels and relief forces: about 2,700-7,000 dead or wounded; Spain: at least 1,700 dead plus thousands of casualties

siege-leiden-1573.gloss: Spanish forces failed to capture Leiden after Dutch rebels flooded the surrounding polders and relieved the city. The victory became one of the revolt's defining episodes.
siege-leiden-1573.casualties: Dutch rebels: about 500 casualties; Spain: about 2,000 casualties

battle-mookerheyde-1574.gloss: Spanish tercios defeated a Dutch rebel mercenary army near Mook. Louis and Henry of Nassau were killed, weakening William the Silent's campaign.
battle-mookerheyde-1574.casualties: Dutch rebels: about 3,000 dead or wounded; Spain: about 150 dead or wounded

fall-antwerp-1585.gloss: Alessandro Farnese captured Antwerp after a long siege and blockade of the Scheldt. The fall transformed trade in the Low Countries and accelerated migration north.
fall-antwerp-1585.casualties: States-General: about 8,000 casualties; Spain: about 1,800 casualties

battle-turnhout-1597.gloss: Maurice of Nassau and Francis Vere caught a Spanish column near Turnhout. Anglo-Dutch cavalry broke the Spanish infantry in a sharp demonstration of Dutch reforms.
battle-turnhout-1597.casualties: Spanish Empire: about 2,000 killed or wounded and 500-700 captured; Dutch Republic and England: 12 killed and 50 wounded

siege-s-hertogenbosch-1629.gloss: Frederick Henry captured the major Spanish stronghold of 's-Hertogenbosch after an ambitious siege and drainage operation. The victory strengthened Dutch control in Brabant.
siege-s-hertogenbosch-1629.casualties: Spain: 1,200 killed and 1,200 diseased or injured; Dutch Republic: unknown

battle-copenhagen-1801.title: Battle of Copenhagen (1801)
battle-copenhagen-1801.gloss: Nelson led a hard-fought attack on the Dano-Norwegian line at Copenhagen. The British victory helped break the League of Armed Neutrality in the Baltic.
battle-copenhagen-1801.casualties: Royal Navy: 274 killed and 737 wounded; Denmark-Norway: estimates vary, including 367 killed, 635 wounded, and 1,179 captured

battle-copenhagen-1807.title: Battle of Copenhagen (1807)
battle-copenhagen-1807.gloss: British forces bombarded Copenhagen to seize the Dano-Norwegian fleet before it could fall under French influence. The attack pushed Denmark-Norway into war against Britain.
battle-copenhagen-1807.casualties: Britain: 42 killed and 145 wounded; Denmark-Norway: military casualties plus about 195 civilians killed and 768 civilians injured

mystic-massacre-1637.title: Mystic massacre
combatants: Connecticut Colony, Narragansett, and Mohegan allies
mystic-massacre-1637.gloss: English colonial troops and Narragansett and Mohegan allies burned the fortified Pequot village at Mystic. The massacre broke Pequot power and became a defining event of the Pequot War.
mystic-massacre-1637.casualties: Pequot: about 400-700 killed; Connecticut, Narragansett, and Mohegan forces: light losses

battle-bloody-brook-1675.title: Battle of Bloody Brook
combatants: New England Confederation and Mohegan allies
battle-bloody-brook-1675.gloss: Native forces ambushed a colonial wagon train and its escorts near South Deerfield. Bloody Brook became one of the sharp early shocks of King Philip's War.
battle-bloody-brook-1675.casualties: Pocumtuc-led Native forces: unknown; New England Confederation and Mohegan allies: at least 58 militia and 16 civilians killed

great-swamp-fight-1675.title: Great Swamp Fight
combatants: New England Confederation and Native allies
great-swamp-fight-1675.gloss: Colonial militia and Native allies assaulted a Narragansett winter fort in the Great Swamp. The attack caused catastrophic Narragansett losses and widened King Philip's War.
great-swamp-fight-1675.casualties: New England Confederation and Native allies: about 70 killed and 150 wounded; Narragansett: about 97 warriors and 300-1,000 noncombatants killed, 300 captured

battle-bushy-run-1763.title: Battle of Bushy Run
combatants: Delaware, Shawnee, Mingo, and Huron forces
battle-bushy-run-1763.gloss: Henry Bouquet's British column fought through Native attacks while moving to relieve Fort Pitt. The victory helped stabilize British positions during Pontiac's War.
battle-bushy-run-1763.casualties: Delaware, Shawnee, Mingo, and Huron forces: about 20-60 killed; Great Britain: about 50 killed and 60 wounded

battle-fallen-timbers-1794.title: Battle of Fallen Timbers
battle-fallen-timbers-1794.gloss: Anthony Wayne's Legion defeated the Northwestern Confederacy near a storm-felled woodland. Fallen Timbers led to the Treaty of Greenville and opened much of Ohio to U.S. settlement.
battle-fallen-timbers-1794.casualties: United States: 33 killed and 100 wounded; Northwestern Confederacy: about 25-40 killed

battle-tippecanoe-1811.title: Battle of Tippecanoe
battle-tippecanoe-1811.gloss: Forces associated with Tenskwatawa attacked William Henry Harrison's camp near Prophetstown. The battle damaged Tecumseh's confederacy and shaped the politics leading into the War of 1812.
battle-tippecanoe-1811.casualties: Tecumseh's Confederacy: about 36-65 killed, 70-80 wounded, and 1 captured; United States: 62 killed and 126 wounded

sand-creek-massacre-1864.title: Sand Creek massacre
sand-creek-massacre-1864.gloss: Colorado volunteers under John Chivington attacked a Cheyenne and Arapaho village at Sand Creek. The massacre became one of the most notorious atrocities of the American Indian Wars.
sand-creek-massacre-1864.casualties: United States: 24 killed and 52 wounded; Cheyenne and Arapaho: estimates range from about 70 to 600 killed

battle-washita-river-1868.title: Battle of the Washita River
battle-washita-river-1868.outcome: United States victory; contested as a massacre
battle-washita-river-1868.gloss: Custer's 7th Cavalry attacked Black Kettle's Southern Cheyenne camp on the Washita River. The action remains disputed as both a battle and a massacre.
battle-washita-river-1868.casualties: United States: 21 killed and 13 wounded; Cheyenne: estimates range from 13 to 150 killed, with 53 women and children captured

battle-rosebud-1876.title: Battle of the Rosebud
combatants: United States, Crow, and Shoshoni allies
battle-rosebud-1876.gloss: Lakota and Cheyenne warriors under leaders including Crazy Horse checked George Crook's column at Rosebud Creek. The fight kept Crook from joining the campaign that soon led to Little Bighorn.
battle-rosebud-1876.casualties: Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho: about 13-36 killed and 63-100 wounded; United States, Crow, and Shoshoni: about 15-36 killed and 43-46 wounded

battle-bear-paw-1877.title: Battle of Bear Paw
battle-bear-paw-1877.gloss: U.S. troops caught the Nez Perce near the Canadian border after a long retreat. Chief Joseph's surrender at Bear Paw ended the Nez Perce War.
battle-bear-paw-1877.casualties: United States: 24 dead and 49 wounded; Nez Perce: 25 killed, 46 wounded, and 431 surrendered or captured

wounded-knee-massacre-1890.title: Wounded Knee Massacre
wounded-knee-massacre-1890.gloss: U.S. 7th Cavalry troops killed Lakota men, women, and children near Wounded Knee Creek. The massacre marked the violent close of the major U.S. Army campaigns against Plains peoples.
wounded-knee-massacre-1890.casualties: United States: 25-31 killed and 33-39 wounded; Lakota: about 250-300 killed and 51 wounded

battle-yatay-1865.title: Battle of Yatay
battle-yatay-1865.location: Yatay Creek near Paso de los Libres, Argentina
battle-yatay-1865.gloss: Triple Alliance forces defeated a Paraguayan column on the Uruguay River. Yatay was the first major land battle of the Paraguayan War and helped reverse Paraguay's early offensive.
battle-yatay-1865.casualties: Triple Alliance: 147 killed and 560 wounded; Paraguay: 1,700 killed, 300 wounded, and 1,200 captured

battle-curupayty-1866.title: Battle of Curupayty
battle-curupayty-1866.gloss: Paraguayan defenders used prepared trenches and artillery to repulse Allied frontal assaults. Curupayty was a severe Triple Alliance setback and slowed the Humaita campaign.
battle-curupayty-1866.casualties: Paraguay: 54 killed and 38 wounded; Triple Alliance: 2,225 Argentine casualties and 2,002 Brazilian casualties

siege-humaita-1867.title: Siege of Humaitá
siege-humaita-1867.gloss: Allied forces besieged the fortress complex guarding the Paraguay River until the Paraguayan garrison evacuated and surrendered. The fall of Humaitá opened the route toward Asuncion.

battle-lomas-valentinas-1868.title: Battle of Lomas Valentinas
battle-lomas-valentinas-1868.gloss: Allied armies defeated Francisco Solano Lopez's main remaining field force during the Pikysyry campaign. The victory opened the road to Asuncion and forced Lopez deeper into the interior.
battle-lomas-valentinas-1868.casualties: Paraguay: 1,500 killed and 1,500 captured; Triple Alliance: 3,270 casualties

battle-acosta-nu-1869.title: Battle of Acosta Ñu
battle-acosta-nu-1869.gloss: Brazilian and Argentine forces destroyed a Paraguayan force that included many boys late in the war. The battle is remembered in Paraguay as the Children's Battle.
battle-acosta-nu-1869.casualties: Paraguay: 2,000 killed and 1,500 wounded or captured; Brazil and Argentina: 182 killed and 420 wounded
